text: {"claims":[{"statement":"The albumen (egg white) contains protein, but little or no fat, and may be used in cooking separately from the yolk.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The albumen (egg white) contains protein, but little or no fat, and may be used in cooking separately from the yolk.","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states the composition of egg white and its possible separate culinary use, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Egg whites may be aerated or whipped to a light, fluffy consistency, and are often used in desserts such as meringues and mousse.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Egg whites may be aerated or whipped to a light, fluffy consistency, and are often used in desserts such as meringues and mousse.","entailment":"The sentence explicitly describes the ability of egg whites to be aerated and their common use in specific desserts, confirming the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Ground eggshells are sometimes used as a food additive to deliver calcium.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Ground eggshells are sometimes used as a food additive to deliver calcium.","entailment":"The quoted text states that ground eggshells can serve as a calcium‑delivering food additive, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Egg yolk becomes a gel, or solidifies, between 61 and 70°C (142 and 158°F).","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Egg yolk becomes a gel, or solidifies, between 61 and 70°C (142 and 158°F).","entailment":"The sentence gives the temperature range at which egg yolk gels, directly supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"gelation of yolk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"61 to 70°C","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Egg white gels at different temperatures: 60 to 73°C (140 to 163°F).","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Egg white gels at different temperatures: 60 to 73°C (140 to 163°F).","entailment":"The quoted passage provides the temperature range for egg‑white gelation, confirming the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"gelation of white","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"60 to 73°C","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Salmonella is killed instantly at 71°C (160°F), but is also killed from 54.5°C (130.1°F), if held at that temperature for a sufficiently long time.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Salmonella is killed instantly at 71°C (160°F), but is also killed from 54.5°C (130.1°F), if held at that temperature for a sufficiently long time.","entailment":"The sentence specifies temperature thresholds for Salmonella death, supporting the claim about its thermal inactivation.","population":"","intervention":"heating to 71°C or 54.5°C","comparator":"","outcome":"Salmonella death","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"71°C instantly; 54.5°C with sufficient time","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Eggs may be pasteurized in-shell at 57°C (135°F) for an hour and 15 minutes.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Eggs may be pasteurized in-shell at 57°C (135°F) for an hour and 15 minutes.","entailment":"The quoted text describes a specific pasteurization protocol for eggs, confirming the claim.","population":"","intervention":"pasteurization at 57°C for 1 h 15 min","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"57°C for 1 h 15 min","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"If a boiled egg is overcooked, a greenish ring sometimes appears around the egg yolk due to changes to the iron and sulfur compounds in the egg.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"If a boiled egg is overcooked, a greenish ring sometimes appears around the egg yolk due to changes to the iron and sulfur compounds in the egg.","entailment":"The sentence links overcooking of boiled eggs with the appearance of a green ring caused by chemical changes, supporting the claim.","population":"boiled eggs","intervention":"overcooking","comparator":"","outcome":"greenish ring around yolk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Overcooking harms the quality of the protein.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Overcooking harms the quality of the protein.","entailment":"The quoted statement directly asserts that overcooking damages protein quality, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"overcooking","comparator":"","outcome":"reduced protein quality","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Peeling a cooked egg is easiest when the egg was put into boiling water as opposed to slowly heating the egg from a start in cold water.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Peeling a cooked egg is easiest when the egg was put into boiling water as opposed to slowly heating the egg from a start in cold water.","entailment":"The sentence compares two cooking methods and states that the boiling‑water method makes peeling easiest, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"boiling water start","comparator":"slow heating from cold water","outcome":"ease of peeling","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Periodic cooking of an egg is the best way to preserve the distinct textures of each part of an egg as well as its nutritional value.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"In February 2025, scientists published research confirming that periodic cooking of an egg is the best way to preserve the distinct textures of each part of an egg as well as its nutritional value.","entailment":"The quoted passage reports scientific findings that periodic cooking optimally preserves texture and nutrition, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"periodic cooking","comparator":"","outcome":"preservation of texture and nutritional value","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"The method requires alternating between boiling and tepid water: two minutes in 100°C (212°F) water, two minutes at 30°C (86°F), repeated eight times.","claimType":"methodological","quote":"The method requires alternating between boiling and tepid water: two minutes in 100°C (212°F) water, two minutes at 30°C (86°F), repeated eight times.","entailment":"The sentence details the procedural steps of the periodic cooking method, providing the method description.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"2 min at 100°C, 2 min at 30°C, 8 cycles","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"The bird's diet affects the flavor of the egg.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"the bird's diet affects the flavor of the egg.","entailment":"The quoted clause directly states that dietary intake by the bird influences egg flavor, supporting the claim.","population":"eggs","intervention":"bird diet","comparator":"","outcome":"egg flavor","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"When a brown-egg chicken breed eats rapeseed or soy meals, its intestinal microbes metabolize them into fishy‑smelling triethylamine, which ends up in the egg.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"when a brown-egg chicken breed eats rapeseed (canola) or soy meals, its intestinal microbes metabolize them into fishy-smelling triethylamine, which ends up in the egg.","entailment":"The sentence describes a causal chain from specific feed to microbial metabolism to the presence of triethylamine in the egg, supporting the claim.","population":"brown‑egg chicken breed","intervention":"eating rapeseed or soy meals","comparator":"","outcome":"triethylamine in egg","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Duck eggs tend to have a flavor distinct from, but still resembling, chicken eggs.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Duck eggs tend to have a flavor distinct from, but still resembling, chicken eggs.","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly compares duck‑egg flavor to chicken‑egg flavor, confirming the claim.","population":"duck eggs","intervention":"","comparator":"chicken eggs","outcome":"flavor distinct yet similar","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"}]}
